mutation.note: mutmut 3.6.0 has no native Windows support ('please use WSL') and this sandboxed session has no WSL available -- confirmed via `python -m mutmut --help`, which hard-blocks with that message before any scope/config is read. No mutation run occurred; quality_ok is honestly false rather than claiming coverage that wasn't measured. The adversarial review below was still performed manually.

gaps.gap: unit tests cover thousands-separator + trailing-zero trimming for a handful of fixed examples, but there is no property test (Hypothesis) over arbitrary Decimal magnitudes/signs/scales -- an edge case like a value with >15 significant digits or a scale that trips Python's Decimal-to-string formatting is unexercised.

the day-granularity fan-out's 'never query beyond the latest completed hour' boundary (today's partial day) is exercised only against mocked get_usage in the unit test, never against a real Postgres testcontainer at exactly the UTC hour boundary -- an off-by-one at midnight/hour-rollover would not be caught.

no test forces ONE of the 11 (or up to 264 for day) concurrent get_usage reads to fail while the others succeed. asyncio.gather without return_exceptions=True will propagate the first exception and abort the whole request (falling into the safe-error path, AC24's shape), but this specific partial-failure behavior is inferred from AC24's generic-error test, not asserted directly against a fan-out.

no test drives N concurrent calls to get_dashboard_reader_principal() before the cache is populated (e.g. the first burst of requests after a cold start / TTL expiry) to confirm it doesn't fire N redundant Secrets Manager + Argon2id verifications -- a thundering-herd cost, not a correctness bug, but unmeasured.

only one injection shape (img onerror) is exercised in one field pair (window_start/metric); a <script>...</script> literal, an event-handler string in delta_text, and injection via the customer_id/metric SELECT options themselves are not separately proven inert -- the CSP + textContent combination make all of these very likely safe, but only the one shape is directly demonstrated end-to-end.

the throttle test proves capacity=1 exhausts after 2 requests, but does not wait out the refill window and assert a subsequent request succeeds again -- only the 'exhausted' half of the token-bucket contract is proven, not the 'refills over time' half.

the Hypothesis property test asserts arbitrary customer_id text never raises an uncontrolled exception, but does not separately fuzz `metric` or `granularity` with the same breadth -- both are covered by fixed-example parametrized tests only.
